observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:

Submit immediately the DVs along with their complete supporting documents to the Audit Team;



Strictly enforce the year-end cut-off for processing of claims to allow sufficient time for the review of submitted documents and preparation of the related financial records; and


Ensure that submission of monthly accounts/transactions are intact and complete within the prescribed period and all claims are thoroughly reviewed for completeness and proper documentation before certifying and endorsing them for payment to avoid delays in the submission thereof to the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management instruct the BAC to conduct a thorough verification of the Class “A” Eligibility Documents listed in Annex “A” of the PhilGEPS Certificate of Registration and Membership to ensure that they are current and updated in order to uphold the veracity, authenticity and validity of the certificate and guarantee that contracts are awarded only to eligible bidders.

We recommended that Management:

Instruct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the BAC Secretariat and Municipal Engineer, to submit the lacking documents for audit and evaluation of the Audit Team and ensure that the contracts and other supporting documents for payment of infrastructures are properly prepared, signed and dated; 

Instruct the concerned municipal employees and officers to conduct a root-cause analysis to effectively address and resolve the late or non-submission of procurement contracts and supporting documents to the Audit Team; and

Instruct the Municipal Engineer to adopt geo-tagging applications for all civil works and infrastructure projects to ensure that geographic coordinates and locations are properly recorded, leading to more transparent, accurate and reliable reports of project accomplishments.  It will also help prevent project duplication and overlapping of projects.  Geo-tagged photos should be attached to the disbursement vouchers as part of the supporting documents.

We recommended that Management instruct the BAC Secretariat to conduct a thorough review of all PRs upon receipt, strictly verifying whether the requested items pertain to specific brand names or contain tailor-fitted specifications that could unduly favor a particular supplier.  If such references or specifications are found, the PR should be returned to the requesting unit for necessary corrections and modifications to ensure compliance with procurement regulations and promote fair competition.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to:

Cause the collection of the deficient business taxes totaling ₱666,794.49 and the corresponding penalties and interests from the concerned contractors; and



Examine the accuracy of the declared gross receipts of taxpayers by considering all available evidence to ensure that tax assessment is accurate and fair.

We recommended that Management:

Strengthen internal controls and reinforce compliance with ethical standards and procurement rules and regulations to ensure that suppliers with potential conflicts of interest, even if indirect, are avoided in future transactions; and


Re-evaluate procurement strategies to prioritize suppliers that are geographically closer and more cost-effective, in order to uphold the principles of transparency, economy and efficiency in government operations.

observations.recommendation:
1.33. We recommended that Management:
1.33.1 Require the OIC-GSO to:
a. strictly comply with the required complete physical count of all
properties at least once a year using the proper format; and
b. Maintain complete Property Cards.
1.33.2 Require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Prepare and maintain complete PPELC;
b. Locate pertinent records to provide details on the recorded
properties in the books which have no details or those with
unaccounted balances;
c. Locate records for the data needed to provide appropriate
depreciation costs of PPE and ensure that every PPE recorded in
the books is provided with depreciation cost, except land account;
d. Prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to effect the necessary
adjustments to correct the misclassified PPE account including
their corresponding Accumulated Depreciation account; and
e. Recognize in the books of accounts the remaining assets recorded
in the registries of public infrastructures under MNGAS.
1.33.3 Instruct the Municipal Accountant and OIC-GSO to reconcile the
differences/variances between the accounting records and the
RPCPPE; and
1.33.4 Require the Municipal Accountant and OIC-GSO as well as other
concern officials to review and study the guidelines and procedures of
COA Circular No. 2020-006 and initiate compliance with the one-time
cleansing of PPE account balances that are verifiable as to existence,
condition and accountability.

2.15. We recommended that Management:
2.15.1 Direct the Municipal Accountant to clear the CIP account by
preparing adjusting entries to record the completed projects in the
appropriate PPE account to reflect the correct CIP account balances
and present reliable account balances in the financial statements;
2.15.2 Instruct the Municipal Accountant to review and analyze previous
records in order to identify the actual projects which were previously
recorded in the CIP account to facilitate the reclassification of these
completed projects to the appropriate PPE account;
2.15.3 Require the Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation costs after
reclassification of the completed projects in order to present fairly the
Government Equity account in the financial statements;
2.15.4 Instruct the Municipal Engineer to conduct regular monitoring of
infrastructure projects of the LGU and prepare a status report of the
same and provide the Municipal Accountant of the said report for the
proper recording and classification of projects in the books of
accounts; and
2.15.5 Require the Municipal Engineer, as the implementing unit, to prepare
and review the Quarterly Report of GPPA to properly confirm and
validate the status of infrastructure projects as indicated in the said
report.

3.20. We recommended that Management:
3.20.1 Direct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee
and the Municipal Accountant with a complete description and cost
segregation of road components for road projects;
3.20.2 Instruct the Inventory Committee Chairman to start the conduct of
inventory of all road networks in the Municipality and prepare the
Report of Physical Count of the Road Network System with complete
details, cost segregation and description for road components of road
projects;
3.20.3 Require the OIC-GSO, Municipal Engineer and Municipal
Accountant to reconcile the inventory of local roads with the recorded
road networks in the books of accounts;
3.20.4 Require the OIC-GSO and Municipal Accountant to prepare and
maintain the Local Road Network Property Card and Ledger Card,
respectively;
3.20.5 Instruct the OIC-GSO to give priority to the MPDO in the assignment
of government vehicle for the use of the latter in the inspection of road
network systems in the Municipality for the completion of LGU’s
Road Map; and
3.20.6 Require the Municipal Accountant to provide proper and complete
disclosures in the NTFS.

5.37. We recommended that Management:
5.37.1 Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Correct the erroneous entry made for the establishment of RPT
receivables and deferred liability accounts for taxes due for CY 2021;
and
b. Exert utmost effort to trace back records and prepare analysis for the
causes of uneven balances of RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred
RPT/SET; and
c. Correct the entry made for advance payments received for CY 2021
and ensure that advance payments of taxes for the succeeding years
should be properly recognized in the books of accounts;
5.37.2 Direct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal
Assessor and Municipal Treasurer, to review and analyze the records
of tax assessment and tax payments as against the RPT/SET
Receivables and Deferred RPT/SET recognized in the books
concerning Sual Power Plant and prepare the necessary adjusting
entries to correct the affected accounts;
5.37.3. Instruct the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer to make an
evaluation of the current system used in their offices relative to the
assessment and collection of taxes and in the production of certified
list of taxpayers for the possible upgrade to a more advanced system
in order that problems and issues could be easily addressed;
5.37.4 Require the Municipal Treasurer to continue on sending demand
letters and notices to delinquent taxpayers; and
5.37.5 Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to make necessary information
drive campaign on the awareness of paying in advance the real
property taxes to encourage the taxpayers of prompt payment;
otherwise, apply the remedies provided under the law in order to
generate more revenue that may be appropriated for the
implementation of additional programs and projects for the benefit of
its constituents.

6.8. We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
coordinate with the concerned AOs to set a higher amount to be withheld
from their salaries for the refund of unexpended cash advances until they
have fully settled the same.

6.9. Likewise, we reiterated the previous year’s recommendation that
Management:
6.9.1 Continue its intensified efforts to demand for the liquidation of
accounts;
6.9.2 Require all AOs to liquidate their cash advances on time; otherwise,
impose administrative sanctions against them for any unjustifiable
reasons due to failure to liquidate cash advances within the prescribed
period; and
6.9.3 Instruct the Municipal Accountant to evaluate the probability of
collection/settlement of accounts of retired and deceased accountable
officers if these warrant the issuance of request for write-off.

7.17. We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management:
7.17.1 Require the PESO to locate the former personnel in-charge with
UNAKA loans and request the transfer of records to determine the
balance of the UNAKA loans a well as the borrowers and to require
the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers;
7.17.2 Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the former PESO Officer to
reconcile the records relative to OCW loan profiles/reports and to
maintain complete and updated individual subsidiary ledgers of all
beneficiaries/recipients of loans for immediate reconciliation to
determine the details of discrepancies and effect adjustments in the
books;
7.17.3 Instruct the Legal Officer to exert utmost effort to send collection
notices to all creditors and adopt efficient and aggressive collection
strategies to enforce collection to recipients/creditors concerned to
generate funds for other constituents who wish to avail of the
livelihood program. If all means failed, resort to legal action in the
collection of overdue receivables to avoid possible loss of government
funds;
7.17.4 Instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert effort to locate the
necessary documents to support the dormant accounts concerned;
7.17.5 Direct the Municipal Accountant and Legal Officer to enforce
collection by sending Demand Letters; and
7.17.6 In case of non-collection, the Management may request for write-off
of the dormant accounts.

8.11. We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that Management:
8.11.1 Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the OIC-GSO to maintain
complete SLCs and SCs respectively, for each item of supplies and
materials or inventory account;
8.11.2 Require the OIC-GSO to consolidate the RIS issued and prepare
SSMI which shall serve as the basis of the Municipal Accountant in
recording the issued supplies as expense;
8.11.3 Consider the creation of Inventory Committee to assist the GSO-
Designate in the inventory-taking process;
8.11.4 Instruct the OIC-GSO/Inventory Committee to conduct periodic
physical inventory of supplies semestrally and to prepare and submit
RPCI to the Audit Team; and
8.11.5 Provide an adequate stockroom for the use of GSO to store the
supplies and materials purchased including equipment which are to
be issued to the office/department needing the same.

8.2. We also recommended that Management require the two consultants for
Management Information System to design a database system or a
computerized inventory system to help in the control and recording of the
receipt and distribution of inventory items.

9.10. We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management:
9.10.1 Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to
determine and identify the specific nature of the unaccounted or
undocumented reconciling items in the BRS of the affected bank
accounts;
9.10.2 Require the Municipal Accountant together with her staff to exert
extra effort in tracing/retrieving the misplaced/missing/unrecorded
disbursement vouchers and other pertinent documents to effect the
necessary adjustments;
9.10.3 Require the Municipal Treasurer to make representation with the
bank officials to obtain copies of the bank’s debit and credit memos to
support the adjustments; and
9.10.4 Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to
review and analyze as well as to locate documents of the unrecorded
reconciling item to be able to determine where the balance of the
Trust Fund was deposited amounting to Ᵽ208,585.99.

10.7. We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:
10.7.1 Review and/or check the total amount being disbursed by the
custodian and see to it that fund balance is reasonably low before
allowing him to replenish his fund; and
10.7.2 Perform cash counts for petty cash fund to assure that balances are
kept intact in the hands/custody of the petty cash custodian.

11.24. We recommended that Management:
11.24.1. Require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Take up in the books of accounts the breeding stock acquired for
the Animal Dispersal Program of the LGU in the total amount of
Ᵽ10,955,000.00;
b. Prepare and maintain WOABSLC; and
c. Adhere with the provisions of IPSAS 27 on the appropriate
accounting of biological asset and the necessary disclosures in
the NTFS to provide additional information on the amount
presented as Breeding Stocks in the financial statements.
11.24.2. Direct the Municipal Accountant, OIC-GSO and Municipal
Agriculturist, to account, trace and provide the existence and/or
dispersal of the 70 and one unaccounted goat and cattle,
respectively, to avoid disallowance in audit; and
11.24.3. Require the GSO to reconcile and maintain an update its
WOABSPC with the Accounting office and OMA;

12.26. We recommended that Management ensure that provisions set forth in the
agreement among the LGU, the farmer associations and the beneficiaries be
strictly implemented in order to sustain and ascertain the continuity of the
project.

12.27. We also recommended that Management:
12.27.1. Direct the Municipal Agriculturist to:
a. Ensure that death of animals be reported to the Audit Team with
complete documentary requirements for the proper relief of
accountability;
b. Require the President of the Farmer Associations concerned to
submit the criteria in the selection of project beneficiaries to the
OMA in order to assess the success of the process of the
identification of beneficiaries, and furnish copy thereof to the
Audit Team;
c. Instruct the Farmer Associations to abide with their duties and
responsibilities, particularly in the submission of quarterly and
special reports in order that efficiency, effectiveness and proper
monitoring of the operation of the project can be achieved; and
d. Strictly monitor the birth of offspring of animals in order to
demand payment equivalent to 50 percent of the book value of
the animals upon reaching the age of 10-12 months for the
continuity of the project.
12.27.2. Demand from the beneficiaries, through the OMA, by issuing a
communication/demand letter for the completion of all the required
documents as listed in the MOA relative to death of animals;
12.27.3. Require all beneficiaries, through the OMA, to insure all animals
under their control and custody with any reputable insurance
company in compliance to the provision of the MOA.

We recommended that Management:
Exert more effort to conduct a complete physical count of all the LGU’s property, plant and equipment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the RPT/SET Receivable accounts, particularly that of Sual Power Plant covering the period 2008-2019, in order to reflect a realistic figure of the balances of the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Accountant prepared Journal Entry Vouchers for adjustment in the books of concerned reconciling items.
observations.reason: The OIC-Municipal Accountant had made adjusting entries only for 2014 and 2015 misstatements. No adjustment made for the other years.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to work back on long outstanding and unaccounted book reconciling items for proper disposition and adjustment and immediately adjust all valid reconciling items per Bank Reconciliation Statement in the books of accounts of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
The OIC-Municipal Accountant made an analysis of the reconciling items.

Adjustments in the books were not yet facilitated.
observations.reason: Though analysis had made, reconciling items were not yet adjusted in the books of accounts to correct book balance of Cash-in-bank account; hence reiterated in Observation No. 5, page 46 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to locate/produce documents of the unidentified bank account to be able to determine where the balance of the Trust Fund was deposited amounting to Ᵽ208,585.99.
observations.managementAction:
No documents were relative to reconciling items were found/located by the Management.

Likewise, no action taken for the undetermined balance of Cash-in-Bank recorded under Trust Fund books.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendations that Management:
Cause the withholding of salaries of the accountable officers concerned until they have fully settled their unliquidated cash advances.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, no action taken by the Management to obtain the consent of Accountable Officers (AOs) with outstanding unliquidated cash advances to withhold their salaries to settle their accounts.
observations.reason: The OIC-Municipal Accountant had made an effort to obtain the consent of one AO and deduction on his salary will take place on April 2021.

observations.recommendation: Require all AOs to liquidate their cash advances on time; otherwise, impose administrative sanctions against them for any unjustifiable reasons due to failure to liquidate cash advances within the prescribed period, in accordance with Section 89 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and CSC No. 0676 dated June 17, 2004.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Even the existence of long outstanding cash advances, the Management had never filed a case as of December 31, 2020; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 6, page 49 of this report.

observations.recommendation: May request for write-off of the unliquidated cash advances of the deceased accountable officers in accordance with the procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: The OIC-Municipal Accountant confirmed that no request has been filed by the Management for the period CY 2020; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 6, page 49 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Ensure that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 and COA Circular No. 2015-002 be strictly complied.
observations.managementAction: No concrete action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Management failed to comply the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: The Accounting Office did not maintain subsidiary records for local roads; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 4,  page 43 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation costs for roads and road components for every road network.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: Depreciation costs were not provided by the Accountant due to lack of details/records of local roads; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 4, page 43 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the GSO to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: The GSO did not maintain local road network property cards and road map; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 4, page 43 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System to be reconciled with the accounting records.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: No Committee was created for the conduct of physical count of local roads; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 4,  page 43 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System to be reconciled with the accounting records.
observations.managementAction: The Management made an effort to prepare the report on the Physical count of the Road Network System.
observations.reason:
The effort of the LGU to prepare the Physical Count of Road Network system is very much appreciated. However, the report is not in the required format, lacks details.

Likewise, no reconciliation made; hence reiterated in Observation No. 4,  page 43 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership over the parcels of land covered by Tax Declarations through Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) in the name of the Municipal Government of Sual.
observations.managementAction: Management had processed the titling of lots.
observations.reason:
The Management had started the processing of the titles of the lots, however, no official record has been released, meaning the titles had not yet finalized. 

The Audit Team had recommended through AOM No. 2021-057 dated February 24, 2021 to make a follow up of the said titles to the government agency concerned in the processing of titles.

observations.recommendation: Create an Appraisal Committee who will render an Appraisal Report which shall serve as the basis for proper valuation of acquired lots.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Appraisal Committee was not created.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous year’s recommendation that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation costs of School Buildings.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Accountant exerted effort to update the depreciation schedules of LGU properties.
observations.reason: Depreciation schedules were updated however, there were still undetermined entries in PPE accounts to which depreciation costs was not provided; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 3, page 42 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Ensure compliance in providing the depreciation costs to every PPE recorded in the books in conformity with the IPSAS 17.
observations.managementAction: Management had partially complied the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Management had not provided depreciation cost on all of its properties; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 3, page 42 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-GSO to prepare the Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties of the agency in accordance with the requirements and the procedures in Sections 76 and 79 of PD 1445 and Section 165 of COA Circular 92-386. Disposal of unserviceable properties generate additional funds, prevent further deterioration and loss of its value and allow the agency to use the space being occupied by these unserviceable properties.
observations.managementAction: Management took action on the inspection of unserviceable properties.
observations.reason:
Though the Management had inspected the unserviceable properties, the disposal of said items was not yet facilitated.

No IIRUP was submitted to the Audit Team and request for inspection of unserviceable properties as at year-end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Create an inventory committee to expedite inventory-taking of supplies.
observations.managementAction: Management did not create the inventory committee.
observations.reason:
No inventory committee was created since the GSO did not maintain supplies on hand. In CY 2020, all supplies were all released to requisitioning department once accepted from the supplier.

For inventory of medicines, the MHO and PCF did conduct their own count of medicines and laboratory supplies on hand.

observations.recommendation: Provide a stockroom or space for the use of Supply Office to keep the supplies and materials to be purchased including equipment which are to be issued to the requisitioning head of department needing the same.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management
observations.reason: A small space was provided but not suitable for large quantities of supplies, therefore the GSO did not maintain inventory stocks and released all procured supplies to requisitioning office.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-GSO to prioritize the conduct of a periodic physical count of its inventories and prepare the report using the prescribed Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to be submitted to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year in accordance with Section 124, Volume I of NGAS for LGUs;
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: For inventory of medicines, the MHO and PCF did conduct their own count of medicines and laboratory supplies on hand.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-GSO to maintain Stock Cards for each specific type of Inventories.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Stocks were not maintained by the GSO; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 10, page 55 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the OIC-GSO to consolidate weekly the RIS for which supplies and materials were issued using the SSMI. The SSMI together with the original copy of the RIS shall be submitted to the Chief Accountant.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: SSMI was not prepared by the OIC-GSO.; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 10, page 55 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly apply the perpetual inventory method in recording inventory purchases and issuance thereof pursuant to Section 114, Volume I of MNGAS Manual for LGUs. In addition, maintain detailed inventory records for each inventory item.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Procurement of supplies was taken up as outright expense. Only procurement of medicines, laboratory/ medical as well as some agricultural supplies were taken up as inventories.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare JEVs on the basis of SSMI.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office used the inventory report of MHO and PCF as their basis in recording expenses.
observations.reason: The basis of the accounting office in recording expenses on medicines was the inventory report prepared by the MHO and PCF which is not in accordance with the accounting guidelines for inventories; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 10, page 55 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to designate an accounting personnel with sense of responsibility to maintain an updated Supplies Ledger Cards for each specific type of Inventories.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Supplies Ledger cards for each specific type of inventories were not yet maintained by the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Exert effort to locate the necessary documents and enforce collection.
observations.managementAction: The Management failed to secure documents relative to dormant accounts.
observations.reason: No documents were secured/ located to facilitate collection of dormant receivables; hence reiterated in Observation No. 9, page 53 of this report.

observations.recommendation: In case of possibility of non-collection, the agency may request for write-off of the dormant accounts in accordance with the requirements and procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 in order to reconcile and clear the books of accounts of dormant receivable accounts and fund transfers for fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management
observations.reason: No request for write-off; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 9, page 53 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to devote time in reviewing the transactions covered by the two reciprocal accounts, Due from and Due to Other Funds, to promptly identify the discrepancies between the two accounts.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Accountant did exert effort to analyze and determine the reconciling items to facilitate the reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts.
observations.reason: Inquiry with the OIC-Municipal Accountant revealed that she devoted time in doing analysis of the two reciprocal accounts; however, she had not determine yet all the reconciling items to make the reciprocal accounts into agreement; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 8, page 52 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Effect the necessary adjustments to bring the two accounts into agreement.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Accountant had exerted extra effort to reconcile the two reciprocal accounts, but no avail.
observations.reason: Despite exerted effort, the reciprocal accounts were still not into agreement as at year-end; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 8, page 52 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the former PESO Officer to reconcile the records relative to OCW loan profiles/reports.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: The records of the PESO and the Accounting Office were still not reconciled; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 7, page 50 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the former PESO Officer to maintain complete and updated individual subsidiary ledgers of all beneficiaries/recipients of loans for immediate reconciliation to determine the details of discrepancies and effect adjustments in the books.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Individual subsidiary ledgers were not maintained by both the PESO and Accounting Office; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 7, page 50 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Locate the former personnel in-charge with UNAKA loans and demand from her the records relative to UNAKA loans and make a reconciliation with the OIC-Municipal Accountant.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Inquiry with the Management disclosed that they did not locate or made a communication with the former personnel-in-charged with the UNAKA loans.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to continue sending collection notices and adopt efficient and aggressive collection strategies to enforce collection to recipients/creditors concerned to generate funds for other constituents who wish to avail of the livelihood program.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Collection notices were not sent to creditors; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 7, page 50 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to employ internal control procedures in order to avoid the recurrence of the same observation.
observations.managementAction: The Treasury Office exerted effort to avoid the occurrence of the same observation.
observations.reason: No concrete internal control procedure was employed by the Treasury Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management: 
Consider the implementation of projects under the Philhealth Capitation Fund and instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to analyze the amount pertaining to PhilHealth Capitation carried in Due to NGAs and make the necessary reclassification entry to properly present the correct account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: The amount pertaining to Philhealth Capitation fund was not yet cleared whether it was already spent or not; hence, reitarted in Observation No. 11, page 58 of this report.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare a subsidiary ledger for all cash donations intended for calamity-related purpose in conformity with COA Circular No. 2014-002;
observations.managementAction: Management complied the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Subsidiary ledgers were not prepared as of December 31, 2020.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to analyze and verify all the funds recorded under the Due to NGAs account and if the same were found to be utilized, prepare liquidation reports and other supporting documents to support the drawing of journal entries in the books of accounts for the liquidation of the fund transfer.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Inquiry with the OIC-Municipal Accountant and review of pertinent records revealed that accounts with unreliable balances were still carried in the books.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to verify the cause of the negative balances and make the proper adjustment to correct the amount presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Analysis and/or verification for accounts with negative balances were not yet made by the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: Review of subsidiary ledgers and trial balances revealed that accounts with negative balances were not yet analyzed and still exiting in the books of accounts as of December 31, 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to implement an optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund. Rigid monitoring of the programs and projects which are identified and prioritized and close coordination with the Municipal Engineering and Municipal Planning and Development Office is enjoined to fully achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes and to ensure timely utilization thereof for the benefit of the constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management partially complied the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: The Management exerted effort to implement the priority projects under the 20% Development Fund, however, the LGU did not optimize the utilization of the fund. There are priority projects which were not yet implemented within the year (2020).

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to require the MDRRMO to ensure strict compliance with the guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and JMC No. 2013-01 of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM and other DRRM related rules and regulations particularly in the preparation of LDRRMFIP of the agency to establish proper management of fund and the regularity of all charges thereto.
observations.managementAction: Management prepared the LDRRMFIP.
observations.reason: The LDRRMFIP was prepared by the Management, however, the format was not in accordance with pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002; hence, reiterated Observation No. 36, page 114 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We also recommended that the MDRRMO submit to the Audit Team the list of projects/activities for CY 2019 charged to the unexpended balance of LDRRM Fund and revised AIP, if changes were made or new expenditure items and/or projects were purchased and or implemented in order for the Audit Team to evaluate properly the charges made by the LGU to the said 5% calamity fund.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: List of 2019 projects charged to unexpended balance was not furnished to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and LDRRMO to submit the same to the auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.
observations.managementAction: Management complied the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Though Management prepared the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM Fund, the report was not submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions set forth in COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 relative to the accounting and reporting guidelines for the LDRRMF of LGUs as well as those National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (NDRRMF) given to LGUs and from other sources.
observations.managementAction: Management partially complied the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Only partial transfer was made/recorded by the Management for the unexpended balance of QRF and MOOE.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the proceeds from the additional one percent tax on real property paid in checks by taxpayer accruing to the SEF shall be automatically released to the local school board once the checks are cleared in the bank and credited in the account of the agency in order that funds shall be available for obligations in the SEF.
observations.managementAction: Management exerted effort to automatically release all proceeds from the additional one percent tax on RPT paid in checks.
observations.reason: Management failed to automatically release all proceeds from the additional one percent tax on RPT paid in checks; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 18, page 72 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to instruct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to release the barangay shares from RPT, including interest thereon to the Barangays, on a quarterly basis within five (5) days after the end of each quarter without any lien or holdback for whatever purpose pursuant to Section 271(d) of RA 7160 and COA Circular No. 92-382.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Though delays in the remittance of RPT shares to the Barangays had decreased, still the Management had not complied with the release of RPT shares within the reglementary period pursuant to Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accounting Office, in cooperation with the Treasury Office, to ensure the complete submission of accounts within the prescribed period for timely examination and verification of accounts pursuant to Section 7.2.1 of the 2009 of the Rules and Regulations on the Settlement of Accounts and Section 39 (1) and (3) of PD 1445 to avoid disciplinary actions and/or penalties under Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.
observations.managementAction: Management exerted effort to submit LGU accounts within the reglementary period.
observations.reason: Management had not submitted LGU accounts within the reglementary period. Management made partial submission per month which affects the verification of the accounts /transactions; hence, reiterated in Observation No. 24, page 87 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Local Chief Executive to: 
Require the OIC-Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and submit monthly SAAOB not later than the twentieth day after the end of each month.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: The budget office failed to submit SAAOB to the Audit Team; hence, reiterated in AOM No. 004 dated January 15, 2021.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the OIC-Budget Officer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to perform quarterly reconciliation of the budget and actual amounts in order to prepare periodically the SCBAA.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason: As per inquiry with the OIC-Municipal Accountant, quarterly reconciliation was not made by her office and the Budget Office.

observations.recommendation: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required year-end financial statements are submitted on or before the 14th day of February of the ensuing year.
observations.managementAction: Management exerted effort to submit FS for CY 2020 on the 14th day of February 2021.
observations.reason: Financial statements were submitted on February 15, however, incomplete. SCBAA was submitted in a latter date.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting entries to record the completed projects in the appropriate PPE account in order to reflect the correct CIP account balances in the financial statements;
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to review and analyze previous records in order to identify the actual projects which were previously recorded in CIP account to facilitate the reclassification of these completed projects to the appropriate PPE accounts; and
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation costs after reclassification of the completed projects.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Continue the conduct of analysis of the PPE account and provide the Audit Team with updated lapsing and depreciation schedules; and
Locate records for the data needed in order to provide appropriate depreciation costs of PPE and ensure that every PPE recorded in the books is provided with depreciation cost, except land account, in conformity with Section 262, Volume I of GAM for LGUs.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Continue analyzing the transactions covered by the two reciprocal accounts, Due from and Due to Other Funds and identify the causes of discrepancies between the two accounts; and
Effect all necessary adjustments to bring the two accounts into agreement.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Exert effort to locate the necessary documents to support the dormant accounts;
Enforce collection by sending Demand Letters; and
In case of non-collection, the Management, through the OIC-Municipal Accountant may request for write-off of the dormant accounts in accordance with procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management:
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to reclassify the recorded ten percent (10%) retention fees relative to infrastructure projects and procurement of goods and services.
The following is the suggested journal entry:
Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable	5,801,334.39
Other Payables			     5,801,334.39
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain Subsidiary Ledger specifically for amount withheld representing retention fee for each Supplier/Contractor.

We recommended that Management:
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to reclassify the musical devices/items to Technical and Scientific Equipment account and provide monthly depreciation cost thereof.
The following is the suggested journal entry:
Technical and Scientific Equipment	63,900.00
Prior Period Adjustment		     63,900.00
Require the OIC-GSO to properly place an inventory tag to all the musical instrument and accessories as well as to issue PAR to the proper custodian of the items to properly identify all government assets and facilitate the conduct of physical inventory.

We recommended that Management require the MSWDO to submit to the Audit Team the copies of valid ID, Barangay Certification with specimen signatures and SAC of beneficiaries for further evaluation of the Audit Team.
We also recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and MSWDO, to submit the following lacking documents to the Audit Team to ascertain the veracity of receipt of financial subsidy, to wit:
Any Identification Documents of recipients concerned with signatures indicated therein; and
Authorization letters from the payee and identification document of the person who received the assistance and signed the payroll other the qualified payee.

We recommended that Management:
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and OIC-GSO to fully account all distributions of supplies and materials relative to COVID-19 response of the LGU to end-users and submit to the Audit Team the distribution list with complete details as to specific item/s and quantity duly received and signed by the beneficiaries;
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to recognize the remaining infrared scanners as inventory in the General Fund books and recognize expenses based on monthly RSMI prepared by the OIC-GSO; and
Instruct the OIC-GSO to maintain Stock Cards on every items of supplies and materials not yet distributed to end-users or requisitioning office/department.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to submit all contracts within (5) five days from its execution thereof as required under COA Circular 2009-001, together with the bidding documents, BAC resolution, minutes of the proceedings, abstracts, and such other documents peculiar to the contract and/or to the mode of procurement which are deemed necessary in the conduct of auditorial review and in the technical evaluation thereof.

We recommended that Management instruct the BAC Chairman and BAC Secretariat to review and discuss the said GPPB Resolution, and to initiate the assessment of 2020 procurement performance of the LGU using the APCPI system and recommend to the HoPE for approval and submission to the GPPB.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to purchase cashbooks to properly record the transactions of those accounts without cashbooks. Likewise, we recommended that the Cash Disbursing Officer/Payroll Master maintain separate cashbook for payrolls under the Trust Fund account.
We recommended further that the Acting Municipal Treasurer ensure to update daily her cashbooks on Cash-in-Bank accounts and certify at month-end each cashbook as to its correctness and completeness.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct all Department Heads to ensure that all DTRs are prepared by all personnel for their review and approval and to submit the same to the Accounting Office for the preparation of payrolls;
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to acknowledge receipt of DTRs before the preparation of payroll based on the actual attendance of employees making reference to time-ins and outs and absences as indicated in every DTRs; and
Require the HRMO to submit to the OIC-Municipal Accountant all DTRs and duly verified and approved accomplishment reports for CY 2020 of all JO workers.

We recommended that Management:
Allow the OIC-Municipal Accountant to review PCVs and its supporting documents before its payment to establish the completeness and appropriateness of supporting documents;
Require the Petty Cash Custodian to certify his cashbook every month end to ascertain the correctness and completeness of the recorded transactions thereto; and
Require all employees through their immediate supervisors or department heads to reimburse their transportation and miscellaneous expenses simultaneously with their claims of travel per diems to ascertain that payment is valid and for official travels only.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the unsubmitted disbursement vouchers, collections reports, payrolls, general journals, bank reconciliation statements and other financial reports to the Audit Team in order to establish the propriety and validity of the transactions and ascertain the completeness of documents.

We recommended that Management:
Require the OIC-MENRO, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, to provide or develop its own sanitary landfill so that it will not depend on private entities for disposal of its residual non-recyclable wastes;
In addition to educating the barangays about upmost observance of proper administration and operation of their existing MRF, instruct the OIC-MENRO to provide support in the creation, maintenance and rehabilitation of barangay MRFs to fully sustain the campaign of the LGU relative to the proper segregation of waste, preparation of recyclable materials for marketing to end-user manufacturers and clean environment; and
Require the OIC-MENRO to continue the operation of its MRF for proper waste disposal and segregation as well as strictly enforce the Anti-Littering Ordinance to maintain cleanliness within the locality to promote public health and green environment.

We recommended that Management instruct the HRMO to encourage the unregistered JO workers to register with the SSS and inform the benefits that can be derived from the SSS Expanded Self-Employment Coverage Program such as protection against hazards of disability, sickness, maternity, old age, death and other contingencies.

We recommended that Management:
Reassess the hiring of consultants and determine their capabilities based on proven expertise, experience and capability. Create a screening committee prior to the renewal of the contracts of service to determine compliance with the qualification standards; and
Limit the number of consultants to those with highly specialized skills and whose functions cannot be performed by those regular employees/head of office/department to avoid unnecessary expenditures and wastage of government resources.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to continue sending notices to delinquent taxpayers and making necessary information drive campaign on the awareness of payment in advance of the RPT in order to encourage the taxpayers for prompt payment; otherwise, apply the remedies provided under the law in order to generate more revenue that may be appropriated for the implementation of additional programs and projects for the benefit of its constituents.

We recommended that Management require the MSWD Officer to assess and evaluate all applicants for financial assistance, including senior citizen, persons with disability or women in emergency and difficult circumstances pursuant to the guidelines promulgated by DSWD in the selection of AICS beneficiaries to strengthen its internal control in the release of AICS and ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the AICS program of the local government.

We recommended that Management:
Facilitate the completion of the GAD Database to help the LGU in its gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation in order to serve the very interest or need of its constituents relative to gender-related issues; and
Instruct the GAD Focal Person to request the Sanggunian Bayan to update the LGU GAD Code to support LGUs efforts in promoting, protecting and fulfilling women’s empowerment and gender equality.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Submit within the reglementary period the Report on Utilization of DRRM Fund to the Audit Team to facilitate review of the charges made to the DRRM Fund;
Maintain the required subsidiary ledgers separately for the unexpended QRF and MOOE balances transferred to the STF identifying the year to which the balance pertain, as well as subsidiary ledgers for the current LDRRMF QRF and MF; and
Maintain Equipment Ledger Card and subsidiary ledgers for properties recorded under the Disaster and Rescue Equipment account.

We recommended that Management require the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP in the prescribed format to establish proper management of fund and the regularity of all charges thereto.
We also recommended that the MDRRMO submit to the Audit Team the list of projects/activities for CY 2020 charged to the unexpended balance of LDRRM Fund for proper evaluation of the charges to the said 5% calamity fund.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Remit the same immediately to avoid misapplication or misuse of the fund as well as to avoid penalties that may be imposed by GSIS, Pag-ibig and PhilHealth for non-remittance within the prescribed period; and
Provide the Audit Team a summary report on the analysis made on Due to GSIS, Pag-ibig and PhilHealth.
Consider the need to increase the number of personnel involved in the conduct of actual physical count of PPE to facilitate its full and complete execution.
