observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to exert more effort to conduct a
complete physical count of all the LGU’s property, plant and equipment and to
consider the need to increase the number of personnel involved in the conduct of
actual physical count of PPE to facilitate its full and complete execution.

Likewise, we recommended that the Management require the OIC-Municipal
Accountant and the OIC-General Services Officer to prepare and maintain the
Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC), Real Property Ledger Card and
Property Cards, respectively, for each class of property, plant and equipment listing
therein the data as to its acquisition, description, custody, estimated life, and other
information about the property, plant and equipment, based on the source documents of
the transactions.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal
Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the RPT/SET Receivable
accounts, particularly that of Sual Power Plant covering the period 2008-2019, in
order to reflect a realistic figure of the balances of the accounts in the financial
statements.

We recommended that the Management instruct the OIC-Municipal
Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to determine and identify the
specific nature of the unaccounted or undocumented reconciling items in the
Bank Reconciliation Statements of the affected bank accounts. Likewise, require
the OIC-Municipal Accountant to work back on long outstanding and
unaccounted book reconciling items for proper disposition and adjustment and
immediately adjust all valid reconciling items per Bank Reconciliation
Statement in the books of accounts of the Municipality.

We also recommended that the Management require the OIC-Municipal
Treasurer and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to locate/produce documents of
the unidentified bank account to be able to determine where the balance of the
Trust Fund was deposited amounting to Ᵽ208,585.99.

We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendations that Management
cause the withholding of salaries of the accountable officers concerned until they
have fully settled their unliquidated cash advances.

Likewise, require all Accountable Officers to liquidate their cash
advances on time; otherwise, impose administrative sanctions against them for
any unjustifiable reasons due to failure to liquidate cash advances within the
prescribed period, in accordance with Section 89 of PD 1445, COA Circular
No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Civil Service Commission No. 0676 dated
June 17, 2004.

Further, the LGU may request for write-off of the unliquidated cash
advances of the deceased accountable officers in accordance with the procedures
stated in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that the
accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management
System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 and
COA Circular No. 2015-002 be strictly complied. Moreover, we recommended
the Management to require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain
subsidiary records and to provide depreciation costs for roads and road
components for every road network and to instruct the General Services Officer
to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local
Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We also recommended the Management to create an inventory committee
to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of
the Road Network System to be reconciled with the accounting records.

We recommended that Management exhaust all possible means to
document the legality of ownership over the parcels of land covered by Tax
Declarations through Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) in the name of the
Municipal Government of Sual and create an Appraisal Committee who will
render an Appraisal Report which shall serve as the basis for proper valuation
of acquired lots.

We reiterated the previous year’s recommendation that Management
instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation costs of School
Buildings and ensure compliance in providing the depreciation costs to every
PPE recorded in the books in conformity with the IPSAS 17.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-General Services
Officer to prepare the Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties to facilitate
the disposal of unserviceable properties of the agency in accordance with the
requirements and the procedures in Sections 76 and 79 of PD 1445 and Section
165 of COA Circular 92-386. Disposal of unserviceable properties generate
additional funds, prevent further deterioration and loss of its value and allow
the agency to use the space being occupied by these unserviceable properties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive create an inventory
committee to expedite inventory-taking of supplies and provide a stockroom or
space for the use of Supply Office to keep the supplies and materials to be
purchased including equipment which are to be issued to the requisitioning head
of department needing the same.

We also recommended that Management:
a) Require the OIC-General Services Officer to:
1. Prioritize the conduct of a periodic physical count of its
inventories and prepare the report using the prescribed Report
of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to be submitted to the
Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year
in accordance with Section 124, Volume I of NGAS for LGUs;
2. Maintain Stock Cards for each specific type of Inventories;
and
3. Consolidate weekly the RIS for which supplies and materials
were issued using the Summary of Supplies and Materials
Issued. The SSMI together with the original copy of the RIS
shall be submitted to the Chief Accountant.
b) Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
1. Strictly apply the perpetual inventory method in recording
inventory purchases and issuance thereof pursuant to Section
114, Volume I of NGAS Manual for Local Government Units.
In addition, maintain detailed inventory records for each
inventory item; and
2. Prepare JEV on the basis of SSMI and designate an accounting
personnel with sense of responsibility to maintain an updated
Supplies Ledger Cards for each specific type of Inventories.

We recommended that Management exert effort to locate the necessary
documents and enforce collection. In case of possibility of non-collection, the
agency may request for write-off of the dormant accounts in accordance with
the requirements and procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2016-005
dated December 19, 2016 in order to reconcile and clear the books of accounts
of dormant receivable accounts and fund transfers for fair presentation of
accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal
Accountant to devote time in reviewing the transactions covered by the two
reciprocal accounts, Due from and Due to Other Funds, to promptly identify
the discrepancies between the two accounts and effect the necessary
adjustments to bring the two accounts into agreement.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that
Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the former PESO
Officer to reconcile the records relative to OCW loan profiles/reports and to
maintain complete and updated individual subsidiary ledgers of all
beneficiaries/recipients of loans for immediate reconciliation to determine the
details of discrepancies and effect adjustments in the books.

Likewise, we recommended that Management should locate the former
personnel in-charge with UNAKA loans and demand from her the records
relative to UNAKA loans and make a reconciliation with the OIC-Municipal
Accountant.

Further, the OIC-Municipal Accountant should continue sending
collection notices and adopt efficient and aggressive collection strategies to
enforce collection to recipients/creditors concerned to generate funds for other
constituents who wish to avail of the livelihood program. If all means failed,
resort to legal action in the collection of overdue receivables to avoid possible
loss of government funds.

We recommended the Management to instruct the OIC-Municipal
Treasurer to see to it that all collectors are properly supervised, their
accountabilities are regularly monitored and require collecting officers to
strictly adhere to the provisions of Sections 29 and 32 of the NGAS Manual,
Volume I.

We also recommended to direct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to revisit
the practices and procedures in handling cash to ensure that all collections are
deposited intact with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the
next banking day.

We recommended the Management to require the OIC-Municipal
Treasurer to strictly monitor the daily remittances of collectors and likewise
monitor the issuance of accountable forms to collecting officers and employ
internal control procedures in order to avoid the recurrence of the same
observation.

We recommended the Management the following courses of action:
 Consider the implementation of projects under the Philhealth
Capitation Fund and instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to
analyze the amount pertaining to PhilHealth Capitation carried in
Due to NGAs and make the necessary reclassification entry to
properly present the correct account in the financial statements;
 Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 particularly for the
recording of funds from private or international donors. Likewise,
effect the necessary adjusting/reclassification entry for the amount of
funds recorded under the General Fund and prepare a Subsidiary
Ledger for all cash donations intended for calamity-related purpose in
conformity with COA Circular No. 2014-002;
 Prepare the corresponding Project Procurement Management Plan
(PPMP) and Annual Procurement Plan (APP) relative to the said
income/fund received from BFP in order that the LGU could properly
utilize the same in accordance with the purpose for which it was
intended through a Supplemental Budget; and
 Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to analyze and verify all the
funds recorded under the Due to NGAs account and if the same were
found to be utilized, prepare liquidation reports and other supporting
documents to support the drawing of journal entries in the books of
accounts for the liquidation of the fund transfer. Likewise, verify the
cause of the negative balances and make the proper adjustment to
correct the amount presented in the financial statements.

We recommended the Management to implement an optimum utilization
of the 20% Development Fund. Rigid monitoring of the programs and projects
which are identified and prioritized and close coordination with the Municipal
Engineering and Municipal Planning and Development Office is enjoined to
fully achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental
outcomes and to ensure timely utilization thereof for the benefit of the
constituents.

We also recommended the Management to require all Municipal
employees concerned to strictly observe and properly utilize the 20%DF in
accordance with JMC N. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended the Management to require the MDRRMO to ensure
strict compliance with the guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 2012-002
dated September 12, 2012 and JMC No. 2013-01 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG and
other DRRM related rules and regulations particularly in the preparation of
LDRRMFIP of the agency to establish proper management of fund and the
regularity of all charges thereto.

We also recommended that the MDRRMO submit to the Audit Team
the list of projects/activities for CY 2019 charged to the unexpended balance of
LDRRM Fund and revised AIP, if changes were made or new expenditure
items and/or projects were purchased and or implemented in order for the
Audit Team to evaluate properly the charges made by the LGU to the said 5%
calamity fund.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal
Accountant and LDRRMO the regular preparation of the monthly Report on
Sources and Utilization of DRRM fund and submit the same to the auditor on or
before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommended that the Management require the MDRRMO to
maximize the utilization of the 5% Calamity Fund allocated for the
implementation of various projects, programs and activities that are envisioned
to help the LGU in strengthening its capacities to effectively and efficiently
manage and possibly reduce disaster risk within the locality pursuant to Section
3 of RA 10121.

We also recommended the Management to strictly follow the provisions
of RA 10121 of the National Disaster Coordinating Council in the
implementation of programs, projects and activities relative to the utilization of
5% LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that the unexpended balance of QRF and MOOE of
LDRRMF for CY 2019 should be transferred to the special trust fund account
maintained by the LGU and record it under the Trust Liabilities-DRRM under
the Trust Fund books in accordance with the aforesaid COA Circular.

We also recommended the Management to strictly comply with the
provisions set forth in COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012
relative to the accounting and reporting guidelines for the LDRRMF of LGUs
as well as those National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund
(NDRRMF) given to LGUs and from other sources.

We recommended that the proceeds from the additional one percent tax
on real property paid in checks by taxpayer accruing to the Special Education
Fund (SEF) shall be automatically released to the local school board once the
checks are cleared in the bank and credited in the account of the agency in
order that funds shall be available for obligations in the Special Education
Fund.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Bids
and Awards Committee strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 12.2 of the
2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and shall prepare the Procurement Monitoring
Report in its prescribed format for approval and submission by the Municipal
Mayor to the GPPB within (fourteen) 14 calendar days after the end of each
semester.

We also recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee strictly
adhere to the provisions of Sections 7.3.5 and 7.4 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA
9184 by preparing and/or submitting to GPPB the final APP within the
stipulated deadline.

We recommended the Management that after the budget proposal has
been approved by the Head of the Procuring Entity and/or other oversight
bodies, the procuring entity’s budget office shall furnish a copy of the budget as
well as the corresponding PPMPs to the BAC Secretariat for its review and
consolidation into the proposed APP. The APP should include not only
common use supplies but all the municipality’s programs/projects/activities.
Likewise, the correct format should be used as prescribed by the GPPB.

We recommended the Management to instruct the OIC-Municipal
Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to release the barangay shares from
RPT, including interest thereon to the Barangays, on a quarterly basis within
five (5) days after the end of each quarter without any lien or holdback for
whatever purpose pursuant to Section 271(d) of RA 7160 and COA Circular
No. 92-382.

We recommended that the Accounting Office, in cooperation with the
Treasury Office, to ensure the complete submission of accounts within the
prescribed period for timely examination and verification of accounts pursuant
to Section 7.2.1 of the 2009 of the Rules and Regulations on the Settlement of
Accounts and Section 39 (1) and (3) of PD 1445 to avoid disciplinary actions
and/or penalties under Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to:
 Require the OIC-Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and submit
monthly Statement of Appropriations, Allotments, Obligations
and Balances not later than the twentieth day after the end of
each month;
 Instruct the OIC-Budget Officer and OIC-Municipal Accountant
to perform quarterly reconciliation of the budget and actual
amounts in order to prepare periodically the Statement of
Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts; and
 Ensure that the required year-end financial statements are
submitted on or before the 14th day of February of the ensuing
year.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Solid
Waste Management Board to exert extra effort to finalize the LGUs’ Solid
Waste Management Plan in accordance with Section 17 of Ecological Solid
Waste Management Act of 2000 and submit the same to the National Solid
Waste Management Commission for approval and implement it accordingly.

We recommended that the Management consider to provide or develop
its own sanitary landfill so that it will not depend on other LGUs or private
entities for disposal of its residual non-recyclable wastes. Likewise, it should
continue to operate its Materials Recovery Facility for proper waste disposal
and segregation as well as strictly enforce the Anti-Littering Ordinance to
maintain cleanliness within the locality and ensure the continuous delivery of
efficient and effective service to its constituents and protection of public health
and environment.

We recommended that the Management strictly adhere to the
aforementioned rules and regulations pertaining to GAD and to:
a) Designate a GFPS-TWG that can devote time and vital effort to lead
proper monitoring of the implementation of GAD-related PAPs and
can suggest corrective measures to improve their implementation.
Also, the designation of the GFPS-TWG Chairperson should be
supported with Memorandum duly signed by the Local Chief
Executive and endorsed by the Department Head concerned; and
b) Involve the LGU-GFPS and its personnel in capacity development
program as mandated in Joint Circular No. 2013-01 to deepen their
knowledge and build their competencies on GAD related laws and
international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis,
gender responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools
among others.

We recommended that the Management must strictly adhere to the
provisions of PCW-DILG-NEDA-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-
01 and ensure that:
a) The Local Sanggunian formulates their GAD Code in coordination
with the LGU GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to support LGUs
efforts in promoting, protecting and fulfilling women’s empowerment
and gender equality; and
b) Establish and maintain the GAD Database to help the LGU in its
gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation in
order to serve the very interest or need of its constituents relative to
gender-related issues and concerns.

We recommended that the Management maximize the utilization of all
its properties in order to obtain maximum benefits from the said projects and
to avoid wastage of government funds.

We also recommended the Management to exert effort to incorporate
other training courses aside from welding to maximize the utilization of the new
Training Facility and enjoy the benefits that can be derived from therefrom.

We recommended that the Management require the OIC-Municipal
Treasurer to continue and improve the collection strategies on taxes due and
collectible by sending RPTOPs and notice of delinquency to taxpayers. If
circumstances warrant, impose the provisions of Sections 258 and 260 of the
Local Government Code of 1991 in the collection of delinquent taxes to satisfy
taxes, penalties and costs with the filing of civil suit with the proper court and
levy on real property and sale of delinquent property or a usable portion
thereof at public auction.

We recommended that regular remittances of taxes withheld be strictly
observed by the LGU in accordance with existing revenue regulations in order
to enable the timely utilization of the much needed fund by the national
government and avoid misapplication or misuse of the fund as well as to avoid
penalties that may be imposed by the BIR for delayed remittances.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit liquidation reports to the national government agencies concerned on the fund transfers which were fully disbursed and make the necessary adjustment to the Due to NGAs and Government Equity accounts for proper presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management had complied with the 2018 recommendation by the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions set forth in Section 157 of GAAM to ensure that only valid claim which is properly supported by sufficient evidence will be taken up as payables and establish the proper authority for its incurrence.
We also recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to examine/analyze and ascertain the validity of long outstanding obligations. Those found valid should be immediately compensated/paid; otherwise, may revert to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund those payables without valid documentation and those which have been outstanding for two years or more and which no actual claim had been filed.
observations.managementAction: Management had complied with the 2018 recommendation by the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Local School Board charge only allowable expenses to the SEF as provided under Section 4 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2017 and refrain from accommodating regular expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has direct mandate to implement. The management, as well as the Local School Board, should adhere strictly with the regulation in the prioritization and utilization of the SEF as required in RA 7160 and the Joint Circular.
observations.managementAction: Management had complied with the 2018 recommendation by the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that OIC-Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to correct the misclassification of accounts in the recording of equipment, inventories and expense accounts for proper presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
We likewise recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure the proper recording of every financial transactions of the Municipality in conformity with PPSAS Chart of Accounts to properly present each and every asset and expense accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management had complied with the 2018 recommendation by the Audit Team. Management made the necessary adjustments to misclassified PPE items.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that OIC-Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant strictly enforce the provisions stipulated in the GAA pertaining to net take home pay of regular employees to promote an efficient and effective public service by ensuring that government personnel have enough to support for their daily needs until the next payday.
observations.managementAction: Management had complied with the 2018 recommendation by the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management continue to appropriate one percent of its Internal Revenue Allotment for the LCPC programs, projects and activities and ensure the maximum utilization of the fund to properly support the LGU’s advocacies on the promotion of the rights and welfare of children and concerned service providers.
observations.managementAction: Management had complied with the 2018 recommendation by the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management continue to appropriate at least 5 percent fund requirement of the total annual appropriations and to incorporate only GAD-related programs and activities in the GAD Plan and Budget to address gender issues and concerns affecting the welfare and full development of women in order to fully achieve GAD commitment on women’s empowerment and gender equality.
We likewise recommended that Management consider the inclusion in its mandate in relation to GAD, the promotion of women’s protection against violence in accordance with Sections 12 and 33 of the IRR of Magna Carta for Women, to secure women against gender-based violence and those in difficult circumstances.
observations.managementAction: The Management followed the 2018 recommendation of the Management to properly allocate and submit Annual Budget for GAD.
observations.reason: The GAD Accomplishment Report (GAD-AR) was not prepared on time. The delayed submission of GAD-AR precluded the Audit Team to assess the gender issues being addressed by the LGU through their PPAs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan to expedite the passage of an ordinance so that the Fish Market Stalls at the Municipal Wharf and Causeway site of Barangay Poblacion could be offered to interested fish vendors and generate additional funds from its operation that could be used to finance its programs and projects beneficial to its constituents.
observations.managementAction: In order that government funds will not be wasted, said Fish Market Stalls at the Municipal Wharf and Causeway site of Barangay Poblacion the Management decided to utilized the structure as deboning area for magdadaing, though not on its original purpose. The façade of the building is also organized as pasalubong center.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure the conduct of complete physical inventory of all municipal properties and the preparation and submission of the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the OIC- Municipal Accountant for reconciliation.  Likewise, require the Property Custodian and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain complete and updated Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards, respectively.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the RPT/SET Receivable accounts, particularly that of Sual Power Plant covering the period 2008-2018, in order to reflect a realistic figure of the balances of the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to determine and identify the specific nature of the unaccounted or undocumented reconciling items in the Bank Reconciliation Statements of the affected bank accounts. Likewise,  require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to produce the corresponding disbursement vouchers of the paid checks as basis of the OIC-Municipal Accountant in making adjustments in the books and order her to account and produce documents where the balance of the Trust Fund was deposited amounting to P208,585.99.
We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant to work back and verify the long outstanding and unaccounted book reconciling items for proper disposition and adjustment, and immediately adjust all valid reconciling items per Bank Reconciliation Statement in the books of accounts of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit liquidation reports to the national government agencies concerned on the fund transfers which were fully disbursed and make the necessary adjustment to the Due to NGAs and Government Equity accounts for proper presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions set forth in Section 157 of GAAM to ensure that only valid claim which is properly supported by sufficient evidence will be taken up as payables and establish the proper authority for its incurrence
We also recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to examine/analyze and ascertain the validity of long outstanding obligations. Those found valid should be immediately compensated/paid; otherwise, may revert to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund those payables without valid documentation and those which have been outstanding for two years or more and which no actual claim had been filed.

We recommended that Management create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count of public infrastructures on the road networks and record in the books of accounts its equivalent amount and disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements pursuant to Items VI (2) and IX (3) of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015. Likewise furnish the audit team with the Report on the Physical Count of Road Network System.
Moreover, instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for each individual networks and the Municipal GSO to maintain a complete Local Road Network Property Card.

We recommended that Management exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership over the parcels of land covered by Tax Declaration through Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) in the name of the Municipal Government and create an Appraisal Committee who will render an Appraisal Report which shall serve as  basis for proper valuation of acquired lots.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation costs of School Buildings account taken up in the books and ensure compliance in providing depreciation cost to every PPE recorded in the books in conformity with the PPSAS 17.

We recommended that Management direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Conduct thorough review, with the involvement of other accounting personnel, and analyze the details of receivables to ascertain their validity and cause the collection of the receivables by sending demand letters to known debtors to facilitate collections; and
Prepare request for write-off of the receivables account balances which were considered dormant and could no longer be collected duly supported with certified relevant documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to reconcile the records with the Municipal personnel concerned handling the UNAKA and OCW loan profiles/reports and both should maintain complete and updated individual subsidiary ledgers of all beneficiaries/recipients of loans for immediate reconciliation to determine the details of discrepancies and effect adjustments in the books.
Likewise, Management continue sending collection notices and adopt efficient and aggressive collection strategies to enforce collection beneficiaries concerned to generate funds for other constituents who wish to avail of the livelihood program.  If all means failed, resort to legal action in the collection of overdue receivables to avoid possible loss of government funds.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to reconcile the reciprocal accounts, Due to Other Funds and Due from Other Funds, in order to promptly identify the discrepancies between the two accounts and effect the necessary adjustments to bring the two accounts into agreement.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant prepare regularly the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM fund and the LDRRMO submit the same to the Audit Team on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 particularly for the recording of funds from private or international donors. Likewise, effect the necessary adjusting/reclassification journal entries for the amount of funds recorded under the General Fund to be transferred to Trust Liability-DRRM account under Trust Fund books and prepare Subsidiary Ledgers for all cash donations intended for calamity-related purpose in conformity with COA Circular No. 2014-002.

We recommended that the proceeds from the additional one percent tax on real property paid in checks by taxpayer accruing to the Special Education Fund (SEF) shall be automatically released to the local school board once the checks are cleared in the bank and credited in the account of the agency in order that funds shall be available for obligations in the Special Education Fund.

We recommended that Local School Board charge only allowable expenses to the SEF as provided under Section 4 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2017 and refrain from accommodating regular expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has direct mandate to implement.  The management, as well as the Local School Board, should adhere strictly with the regulation in the prioritization and utilization of the SEF as required in RA 7160 and the Joint Circular.

We recommended that Management consider the implementation of projects under the Philhealth Capitation Fund and instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to analyze the amount pertaining to PhilHealth Capitation recorded as Due to NGAs account in the books of accounts and make the necessary reclassification entry to properly present the account in the financial statements.

We recommended that OIC-Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to correct the misclassification of accounts in the recording of equipment, inventories and expense accounts for proper presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
We likewise recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure the proper recording of every financial transactions of the Municipality in conformity with PPSAS Chart of Accounts to properly present each and every asset and expense accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Bids and Awards Committee strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 12.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report in its prescribed format for approval and submission by the Municipal Mayor to the GPPB within (fourteen) 14 calendar days after the end of each semester.

We recommended that Management provide or develop its own engineered sanitary landfill so that it will not depend on other LGUs or private entities for disposal of its residual non-recyclable wastes.  Likewise, Management continues to operate its Materials Recovery Facility for proper waste disposal and segregation as well as strictly enforce the Anti-Littering Ordinance to maintain cleanliness within the locality.  Availability of the facilities will ensure the continuous delivery of efficient and effective service to its constituents and protection of public health and environment.

We recommended that Accounting Office, in cooperation with the Treasury Office, ensure the complete submission of accounts within the prescribed period for timely examination and verification of accounts pursuant to Section 7.2.1 of the 2009 of the Rules and Regulations on the Settlement of Accounts,   Section 39 (1) and (3) of P.D 1445, thus to avoid disciplinary actions and/or penalties pursuant to Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.

We recommended that OIC-Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant strictly enforce the provisions stipulated  in the GAA pertaining to net take home pay of regular employees to promote an efficient and effective public service by ensuring that government personnel have enough to support  for their daily needs until the next payday.

We recommended that Management continue to appropriate one percent of its Internal Revenue Allotment for the LCPC programs, projects and activities and ensure the maximum utilization of the fund to properly support the LGU’s advocacies on the promotion of the rights and welfare of children and concerned service providers.

We recommended that Management continue to appropriate at least 5 percent fund requirement of the total annual appropriations and to incorporate only GAD-related programs and activities in the GAD Plan and Budget to address gender issues and concerns affecting the welfare and full development of women in order to fully achieve GAD commitment on women’s empowerment and gender equality.
We likewise recommended that Management consider the inclusion in its mandate in relation to GAD, the promotion of women’s protection against violence in accordance with Sections 12 and 33 of the IRR of Magna Carta for Women, to secure women against gender-based violence and those in difficult circumstances.

We recommended that Management coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan to expedite the passage of an ordinance so that the Fish Market Stalls at the Municipal Wharf and Causeway site of Barangay Poblacion could be offered to interested fish vendors and generate additional funds from its operation that could be used to finance its programs and projects beneficial to its constituents.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to exert effort in the collection of taxes due and collectible and ensure the strict compliance of the provisions of Sections 258 and 260 of the Local Government Code in the collections of delinquent taxes.

We recommended that Management continue to collect/deduct and remit taxes due to BIR.  Likewise, the unremitted balance for CY 2018 of Ᵽ22,747.53 as of January 31, 2019 should be remitted immediately to avoid surcharge/penalties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict compliance in the proper handling and remittance of collections and that the same are deposited intact to government depository banks in accordance with the requirements of PD 1445 and the MNGAS for LGUs to avoid misuse or misappropriation of public funds.

   adjust all valid reconciling items per Bank Reconciliation  Statement  in
   the books of accounts of the Municipality.

   of the agency in accordance with Sections 76 and 79 of  PD  1445.Disposal
   of these properties may:


        a. generate additional  funds,  when  sold,  to  be  used  for  the
           agency’s operations;


        b. relieve the responsible official  of  accountability  over  said
           properties;


        c. prevent said unserviceable properties from further deterioration
           and loss of value; and


        d. allow the agency to  use  the  space  being  occupied  by  these
           unserviceable properties.

           Municipal Government of Sual and

   Joint Circular.

   support of the ECCD Program.

   tax on real property paid in checks accruing to  the  Special  Education

   once the checks are credited in order that funds shall be available  for
   obligations incurred out of the Special Education Fund.

   Revenue Regulations No. 2-98 as amended by Revenue  Regulations  No.  10-
   2008 in withholding of taxes on all compensations being received  by  the
   officials and employees of the  Municipality,  including  honoraria,  and
   remit the same to BIR in accordance with said regulations.

   of all contracts/purchase orders for review together with the  supporting
   documents entered into by the municipality  within  five  days  from  the
   execution of the contract in order to detect/correct any  defects  and/or
   deficiencies thereon immediately and to  prevent  any  possible  loss  of

   Municipal Treasurer to open a separate  bank  account  intended  for  the
   transferred unexpended balances of LDRRMF for proper  monitoring  of  the

We recommended that the  Municipal  Accountant  submit  liquidation

   necessary adjustment to the Due to NGAs and  Government  Equity  accounts

   submission  of  reports   and   supporting   documents   evidencing   the

   No. 2009-006 which requires submission within the first ten (10) days  of

We recommended that the Municipality continue  to  appropriate  one
   percent of its Internal Revenue  Allotment  for  the  LCPC  programs  and
   activities  and  to  fully  maximize  the   funds   allocated   for   the
   implementation of  the  programs,  projects  and  activities  that  would

   children.


        Likewise, the target beneficiaries for every program or activity to

   least 5 percent fund requirement of the total annual  appropriations  and

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to intensify/improve their collection strategies on taxes due and collectible by sending RPTOP’s and notice of delinquency to taxpayers and forming committee to conduct an information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes, and if circumstances warrant, impose the provisions of Sections 258 and 260 of the Local Government Code of 1991 in the collections of delinquent taxes to satisfy taxes, penalties and costs with the following:

Seizure of personal property of the taxpayer and sale thereof at public auction;
File civil suit with the proper court; and
Levy on real property and sale of delinquent property or a usable portion thereof at public auction.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 | For CY 2016, only P267,305.64 was collected from 14 delinquent taxpayers of different barangays which is very minimal considering the huge amount involved.
observations.reason:
Collection made was minimal because inquiry from the Treasury personnel disclosed that Notices of Delinquency were prepared, however, these were not sent to concerned taxpayers in 2016.

Reiterated in Observation No. 17, page 56 of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipality continue to appropriate at least 5 per cent fund requirement in the annual budget proposal and to incorporate only GAD-related programs and activities in the GAD Plan to address gender issues and concerns affecting the welfare and full development of women in order to eradicate gender-based inequalities and enable women and men to equally contribute and benefit from development. 

We have also recommended that management fully maximize GAD budget in implementing GAD–related programs and activities in order to attain maximum fulfillment of its objectives.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 | Honorarium and financial assistance given to senior citizens were already excluded from the GAD budget of 2016 and a separate budgetary account was provided for said purpose.
observations.reason:
However, per verification made, there were still expenditures charged to GAD budget which were not GAD-related.
